coredata.dc:title: A core–shell structure ratiometric fluorescent probe based on carbon dots and Tb3+ for the detection of anthrax biomarker
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Heng, Hui
Ma, Deming
Gu, Qingyang
Li, Jinyan
Jin, Haibo
Shen, Ping
Wei, Jiaxin
Wang, Ziwei
coredata.dc:description: A novel core–shell structure ratiometric fluorescent probe was developed, which can selectively and sensitively detect 2,6-dipicolinic acid (DPA) as an anthrax biomarker. Carbon dots (CDs) was embedded into SiO2 nanoparticles, which was acted as an internal reference signal. Tb3+ with green emission was connected to the carboxyl functionalized SiO2, which was acted as a responsive signal. With the addition of DPA, the emission of CDs at 340 nm was unchanged, while the fluorescence of Tb3+ at 544 nm was enhanced by the antenna effect. In the concentration range of 0.1–2 μM, the fluorescence intensity ratio of I 544/I 340 showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of DPA, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 10.2 nM. In addition, the dual-emission probe showed an obvious fluorescence color change from colourless to green with increasing DPA under UV light, which enabled visual detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Core–shell structure
Anthrax biomarker
Carbon dots
Tb3+
Ratiometric fluorescent probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Luminescence properties of CDs and CDs@SiO2-Tb

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM image of CDs@SiO2 (A) and CDs@SiO2-Tb(B); DLS of CDs@SiO2 (C) and CDs@SiO2-Tb (D) in water; TEM image of CDs@SiO2-Tb (E) and EDS of CDs@SiO2-Tb (F).

(A) The wide XPS spectra of CDs@SiO2-Tb; (B) XPS spectra of CDs@SiO2-Tb for Tb 3d; (C) XPS spectra of CDs@SiO2-Tb for C 1 s; (D) XPS spectra of CDs@SiO2-Tb for Si 2p；(E) XPS spectra of CDs@SiO2-Tb for O 1 s.
(A) FT-IR spectra of CDs (a), CDs@SiO2 (b), CDs@SiO2-COOH (c) and CDs@SiO2-Tb (d); (B) Zeta potentials of CDs, CDs@SiO2, CDs@SiO2-COOH and CDs@SiO2-Tb.

(A) The optimal excitation wavelength of CDs@SiO2-Tb with DPA (1 μM) under different excitation wavelength (λ ex = 265, 270, 275, 280, 285 nm); (B) The emission spectra of CDs (a) and CDs@SiO2-Tb (b) under λ ex = 275 nm.

(A) The emission spectra of CDs@SiO2-Tb with increasing DPA concentration (λex = 275 nm)；(B) Luminescence intensities of 340 and 544 nm in CDs@SiO2-Tb as a function of the DPA concentration; (C) Linear relationship between DPA concentration and the fluorescence ratio(I 544/I 340) of CDs@SiO2-Tb in the concentration range of 0.1–2 μM；(D) CIE chromaticity diagram of CDs@SiO2-Tb at different concentrations of DPA under the excitation of 275 nm; (E) The fluorescence color of CDs@SiO2-Tb with the concentration of DPA in 5-50 μM under 254 nm UV light.
(A) Photoluminescence decay curve of the CDs@SiO2-Tb without DPA; (B) Photoluminescence decay curve of the CDs@SiO2-Tb with 1 μM DPA. (excited under 275 nm and monitored at 544 nm).

Fluorescence responses of CDs@SiO2-Tb toward different potentially interfering substances. The purple bars represent an addition of 1 μM of interfering acid or ion to CDs@SiO2-Tb. The red bars represent a subsequent addition of 1 μM DPA to the solution. (λ ex = 275 nm).
Schematic illustration of preparation process of CDs@SiO2-Tb.
Detection of DPA in fetal bovine serum by CDs@SiO2-Tb.

head.ce:title: A core–shell structure ratiometric fluorescent probe based on carbon dots and Tb3+ for the detection of anthrax biomarker

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of New Materials and Chemical Engineering, Beijing Institute of Petrochemical Technology, Beijing 102617, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of New Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of New Materials and Chemical Engineering, Beijing Institute of Petrochemical Technology, Beijing 102617, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory of Fuels Cleaning and Advanced Catalytic Emission Reduction Technology, Beijing 102617, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Fuels Cleaning and Advanced Catalytic Emission Reduction Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Key Laboratory of Fuels Cleaning and Advanced Catalytic Emission Reduction Technology, Beijing 102617, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of New Materials and Chemical Engineering, Beijing Institute of Petrochemical Technology, Beijing 102617, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of New Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel core–shell structure ratiometric fluorescent probe was developed, which can selectively and sensitively detect 2,6-dipicolinic acid (DPA) as an anthrax biomarker. Carbon dots (CDs) was embedded into SiO2 nanoparticles, which was acted as an internal reference signal. Tb3+ with green emission was connected to the carboxyl functionalized SiO2, which was acted as a responsive signal. With the addition of DPA, the emission of CDs at 340 nm was unchanged, while the fluorescence of Tb3+ at 544 nm was enhanced by the antenna effect. In the concentration range of 0.1–2 μM, the fluorescence intensity ratio of I 544/I 340 showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of DPA, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 10.2 nM. In addition, the dual-emission probe showed an obvious fluorescence color change from colourless to green with increasing DPA under UV light, which enabled visual detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacillus anthracis is one kind of infectious bacteria which causes anthrax [1–3]. The Bacillus anthracis spores can live even in extreme conditions, such as high pressure, high temperature, strong acidity and basicity. In addition, the Bacillus anthracis spores can spread through food, water, and air [2,4,5]. 2,6-dipicolinicacid (DPA) is a major biomarker of Bacillus anthracis spores, which has a percentage of 5% –15 % of the dry mass [6–8]. Among the reported DPA detection methods, fluorescence detection technology has been favored due to its rapid response, high sensitivity, and good selectivity [7,9,10].

Rare-earth ions are widely used in fluorescence detection due to the unique luminescence properties, which show high fluorescence intensity, long fluorescence lifetime, and large Stokes shift [11–14]. The fluorescence sensors based on rare-earth ions have been extensively studied. As DPA can absorb energy and transfer it to rare-earth ions through the antenna effect, which can enhance the fluorescence of rare-earth ions [3,15,16]. In recent years, lots of functionalized nanomaterials based on rare-earth ions have been used in the detection of DPA. Li et al developed a covalent organic framework (COF) material based on europium functionalization as a probe for the detection of DPA and showed good performance in terms of high sensitivity and specific recognition [17]. Chen et al reported a metal–organic framework (MOF) based on Eu3+ and Tb3+, which can detect DPA and Cu2+ through a one-step method [18]. Yin et al developed a dual-mode platform of ratio colorimetry and fluorescence based on Eu3+ modified silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) for rapid, sensitive and intuitive detection of DPA [19]. Lei et al synthesized a multi-ratio fluorescence sensor based on bimetallic lanthanide metal–organic framework with Eu3+ and Tb3+, which had multiple recognition sites for DPA [20].

In addition, compared with single fluorescent probes, ratiometric fluorescent probes can eliminate the environmental fluctuation and background interference by introducing an internal referenced signal and a responsive signal [21]. Our research group first reported a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe based on rare-earth nanosheets for the detection of DPA [22–24].

Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted the attention in various fields because of the unique properties, such as excellent optical properties, low toxicity and good biocompatibility [25]. In addition, CDs are easy to obtain and modify. Rare-earth ions functionalized CDs have been designed to detect DPA in the literatures [9,26,27]. However, CDs are easily affected by environmental factors due to the complex functional groups on the surface, which may be unstable in the detection environment.

Core-shell structure is an excellent structural scheme to improve the chemical, catalytic, and luminescent properties. The core and the shell can be combined by chemical or physical methods, which shows better properties than the counter parts. In the detection, SiO2 is usually used as the shell to prepare a fluorescent probe as it is chemically inert and optically transparent, which can protect the core from environmental interference [28]. In addition, the surface of silica is easily modified, and the dispersibility in water can be improved [29].

In this study, a core–shell ratiometric fluorescent probe (CDs@SiO2-Tb) was developed. CDs was as the core and its fluorescence was acted as the reference signal. SiO2 was used as the shell to coat the CDs, which was functionalized by hydroxylation and further carboxylation. And then, Tb3+ was connected with carboxyl, which was used as the responsive signal. The prepared ratiometric fluorescent probe integrates two emission centers in one platform, which can detect DPA selectively and sensitively.

Firstly, CDs was synthesized by hydrothermal method [30] at 180 °C for 6 h in a Teflon-autoclave. Secondly, CDs was coated with SiO2 by reverse microemulsion method [31] to obtain CDs@SiO2. Then CDs@SiO2 was functionalized by hydroxylation and further carboxylation to obtain CDs@SiO2-COOH. Finally, Tb3+ was coordinated with carboxyl to obtain CDs@SiO2-Tb (Scheme 1 ). The supporting information contains detailed experimental methodologies.

0.1 mL of CDs@SiO2-Tb solution was mixed with 2 mL of DPA solution at different concentration (0.1 ∼ 2 μM). Then, shaking vigorously for 10 s at room temperature, the relevant luminescence values were collected by a fluorescence spectrophotometer under λ ex = 275 nm. To ensure the precision of the measurement, all experimental data were collected in triplicate and the same approach was used to investigate selectivity and ion interference at a DPA concentration of 1 μM.

SEM micrographs of CDs@SiO2 and CDs@SiO2-Tb were shown in Fig. 1 A and B. CDs@SiO2 were smooth spherical particles with a diameter of about 310 nm, and when Tb3+ was connected to the SiO2 layer, the diameter of CDs@SiO2-Tb was increased, and the surface became rough. From the dynamic lightscattering (DLS), the size distribution of CDs@SiO2 and CDs@SiO2-Tb can be observed in Fig. 1C and D. The changed in diameter were from 312 nm for CDs@SiO2 to 370 nm for CDs@SiO2-Tb, which was consistent with the SEM results. CDs was coated with SiO2 layer to form a core–shell structure, which can be observed in the TEM image (Fig. 1 E and Fig. S1). The EDS results of CDs@SiO2-Tb (Fig. 1F) suggested the existence of Tb, C, Si, O in the core–shell structure and the elemental composition was in agreement with the feed ratios.

XPS has been used to analyze the elemental composition and chemical states of CDs@SiO2-Tb. The characteristic peaks attributed to C1s (284 eV), O1s (531 eV), Si2p (102 eV) and Tb3d (1277 eV) can be seen from the wide spectrum (Fig. 2 A). According to the partial peak fitting of C1s (Fig. 2C), the three peaks were respectively located at 287.6 eV, 285.9 eV and 284.4 eV, corresponding to C = O, C-O and C–H respectively. By peaking Si2p (Fig. 2D), 103.2 ev and 101.8 ev can be obtained, both of which corresponded to O-Si-O. The peak at 531.6 eV in the O1s spectrum came from O-Si and corresponded to the chemical bond of infrared (Fig. 2E). However, the peak signal of Tb3d (Fig. 2A) was very small, which may be caused by the low concentration of Tb3+ in the sample [32].

The surface modification process of CDs@SiO2-Tb was analyzed by FT-IR and zeta potential. As shown in Fig. 3 A-a, O–H and C-O stretching vibrations of CDs appeared at 3420 cm−1 and 1100 cm−1. Stretching vibrations of C = O and C = C appeared at 1650 cm−1. In addition, the stretching vibration at 2945 cm−1, 1398 cm−1 and 2100 cm−1 can be ascribed to C–H and C = N, respectively. For the CDs@SiO2 (Fig. 3A-b), the characteristic peak of Si-O-Si bond was at 1108 cm−1. The absorptions at 1650 cm−1 and 3420 cm−1 could be ascribed to the stretching vibration of C = O and O–H of CDs [33–35]. For the CDs@SiO2-COOH (Fig. 3A-c), the band at 1660 cm−1 corresponded to the bending vibration of C = O in –COOH, which indicated the presence of a carboxyl group and thus demonstrated the successful carboxylation of CDs@SiO2 [36]. For CDs@SiO2-Tb (Fig. 3A-d), the emission of carboxyl at 1653 cm−1 showed a blue shift, indicating that Tb3+ coordinated with carboxyl group [37].

In addition, the zeta potential of CDs (Fig. 3B) changed from 16.5 mV to −12.4 mV after SiO2 coating. The potential value changed from −12.4 mV to −21.3 mV due to the surface carboxylation. The zeta potential of CDs@SiO2-Tb was 13.2 mV, indicating the successful preparation of CDs@SiO2-Tb.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence properties of CDs and CDs@SiO2-Tb
ce:section.ce:para: The emission spectra of CDs@SiO2-Tb with DPA (1 μM) under different excitation wavelengths (λ ex = 265, 270, 275, 280, and 285 nm) was shown in the Fig. 4 A. The emission intensity of characteristic Tb3+ at 544 nm reached the maximum under λ ex = 275 nm. Therefore, 275 nm was selected as the optimal excitation wavelength. The emission spectra of CDs and CDs@SiO2-Tb under the excitation wavelength of 275 nm was shown in Fig. 4B. CDs (Fig. 4B-a) showed strong emission at 340 nm. For CDs@SiO2-Tb (Fig. 4B-b), there existed CDs emission at 340 nm and Tb3+ characteristic emission (5D4→7F5) at 544 nm.

The fluorescence response of CDs@SiO2-Tb with DPA was shown in Fig. 5 A (λ ex = 275 nm). With the addition of DPA, the emission intensity of CDs remained unchanged, which was acted as the internal reference. However, the Tb3+ emission (544 nm) showed a sharp increase with DPA, which was acted as the response signal (Fig. 5B). The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 10.2 nM (LOD = 3S b/m, where m was the slope of the calibration curve and S b was the standard deviation of the blank area). The LOD was 6 × 103 times lower than the infective dose of Bacillus anthracis (6 × 10-5 M)[26,38]. It is still a better result than the previous reported work of detecting DPA based on Tb3+ (Table S1). In the range of 0.1–2 μM, the emission intensity ratio (I 544/I 340) and the DPA concentration showed a good linear relationship (R 2 = 0.998), and the linear equation was y = 0.74x + 0.08 (Fig. 5C). As shown in the Fig. 5D, the CIE chromaticity diagram showed the change in luminescence color from blue to green after adding DPA under the excitation wavelength of 275 nm. Under the irradiation of UV light (254 nm), with the continuous increase of DPA concentration, the fluorescence color gradually changed from colorless to bright green within the range of 5 μM to 50 μM (Fig. 5E), which showed the potential to realize visual detection.

As shown in Fig. 6 , fluorescence lifetime was measured under λ ex = 275 nm and collected at λ em = 544 nm. The fluorescence lifetime decay curve was fitted by single-exponential equation I (t) = I 0 exp (-t/τ), where I 0 was the fluorescence intensity at time zero, I (t) was fluorescence intensity change with time, and τ was the lifetime [39]. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs@SiO2-Tb with a concentration of 1 μM DPA (τ = 0.75 ms) was longer than that of CDs@SiO2-Tb without DPA (τ = 0.66 ms).

As SiO2 shell can improve the dispersibility of CDs@SiO2-Tb in water, which increase the number of recognition sites and lead to more Tb3+ contact with DPA, thus generating stronger fluorescence signals. As the coordination between CDs@SiO2-COOH and Tb3+ is not unsaturated, and water molecules occupy the vacant coordination center of Tb3+, leading to the quenching emission of Tb3+. In addition, DPA has higher metal–ligand chelation coordination and cation interaction with rare-earth ions due to its unique structure, which can replace water molecules to chelate with Tb3+, eliminating the influence of the water quenching effect. It is generally known that the low molar extinction coefffcient and poor luminescence efficiency of rare-earth ions, so the sensitized luminescence effect requires an efficient energy transfer from the activator to the acceptor. DPA can absorb and transfer energy to Tb3+ through the antenna effect, thus enhancing the fluorescence [11,13].

In order to validate of analytical method, the intra-day and inter-day precision and accuracy values have been summarized in Table S2. The relative standard deviation (RSD) values for intra-day precision and inter-day precision accuracy of the probe for DPA were 1.33%-2.36% and 1.14%-2.14%, respectively. The relative errors or percentage-bias for intra-day and inter-day accuracy were −0.592%-0.582% and 0.123%-0.319%, respectively.

The practical application of CDs@SiO2-Tb was evaluated by adding different concentration of DPA into fetal bovine serum solution. As shown in Table 1 , the recovery rate and RSD were respectively 97.1%-103.6% and 0.32%-1.14%. The detection of DPA in tap water was also conducted (Table S3), the recovery rate and RSD were 94.27%-102.4% and 0.3%-2.1%, respectively, indicating that the fluorescence detection method can be used to detect DPA in real samples.

In conclusion, a novel core–shell structure ratiometric fluorescent probe for DPA detection was designed. CDs was as the core and coated with SiO2 layer, and then Tb3+ was successfully connected to SiO2 shell after carboxylation. The fluorescence of CDs was stable with the protection of SiO2 shell, which can be acted as a reference signal. The Tb3+ emission exhibited a sharp increase with DPA concentration, which was used as a response signal. In the concentration range of 0.1–2 μM, the DPA concentration was linearly proportional to the emission intensity of Tb3+ at 544 nm, and the LOD was 10.2 nM. In addition, the fluorescence color gradually changed from colorless to bright green with DPA in the concentration range of 5–50 μM, enabling the visible detection. It is worth noting that the probe can also be applied to the detection of DPA in real sample. This work can provide a simple, efficient and accurate method for DPA detection.

Hui Heng: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Deming Ma: Validation, Formal analysis. Qingyang Gu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Jinyan Li: Validation. Haibo Jin: Conceptualization. Ping Shen: Formal analysis. Jiaxin Wei: Formal analysis. Ziwei Wang: Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Beijing Natural Science Foundation (2232051), R&D Program of Beijing Municipal Education Commission (KM202110017008), and Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by BAST (BYESS2023258), the Beijing Undergraduates Research Training Program (URT: 2021J00086).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122793.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Chem. 92 (2020) 12934-12942.
